criteria: The agent has read the Introduction completely to understand the motivation for explicitly incorporating bond-angle information via line graph neural networks, in contrast to distance-only GNNs such as CGCNN, MEGNet, and SchNet.
The agent has read the 'Atomistic graph representation' section to understand the periodic 12-nearest-neighbor crystal graph construction and its node/edge feature definitions.
The agent has read the 'Atomistic line graph representation' section to understand how the line graph's nodes correspond to bonds and its edges correspond to bond-angle triplets.
The agent has read the 'Edge gated graph convolution' section and Equations 1-6 to understand how node and edge features are jointly updated using normalized edge gates.
The agent has read the 'ALIGNN update' section, Equations 7-8, and Fig. 2 to understand how line-graph convolution and bond-graph convolution alternate within a single ALIGNN layer.
The agent has read the 'Overall model architecture and training' section and Table 1 to understand the default ALIGNN model configuration.
The agent has read the 'Model performance' section and Tables 2-5 to understand the datasets, splits, and comparison baselines used to evaluate ALIGNN.
The agent has read the 'Model analysis' section together with Table 6, Fig. 3, and Fig. 4 to understand the layer ablation, accuracy-cost tradeoff, and learning-curve studies.
The agent has read the Methods section ('ALIGNN model implementation and training') to understand the exact training hyperparameters, loss functions, and hardware used.

The agent has read the Data Availability and Code Availability statements to understand that datasets are accessed via the JARVIS-Tools figshare module and that the reference implementation is hosted on GitHub at usnistgov/alignn.
The plan includes constructing a periodic crystal graph using a 12-nearest-neighbor rule per atom, expanded to include all atoms within the full neighbor shell of the 12th-nearest neighbor.

The plan includes assigning each atom node a feature vector derived from elemental properties (electronegativity, group number, covalent radius, valence electrons, first ionization energy, electron affinity, block, atomic volume) inspired by CGCNN.
The plan includes representing initial bond (edge) features as an RBF expansion of interatomic distances, with cutoff support of 0-8 Å for crystals and up to 5 Å for molecules.
The plan includes constructing the atomistic line graph whose nodes are the bonds of the atomistic graph and whose edges connect bond pairs that share a common atom.
The plan includes computing initial line-graph edge features as an RBF expansion of the bond-angle cosines using the arccos formula on atomic displacement vectors.
The plan includes implementing a reusable edge-gated graph convolution module that updates both node and edge representations, to be applied to both the bond graph and the line graph.
The plan includes composing one ALIGNN layer as a line-graph edge-gated convolution followed by a bond-graph edge-gated convolution that consumes the line-graph's bond messages as edge input.
The plan includes stacking N=4 ALIGNN layers followed by M=4 plain edge-gated GCN layers, then global average pooling and a single linear output layer, matching Table 1.
The plan includes using SiLU (Swish) activations throughout the network instead of ReLU or Softplus.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing the Materials Project (2018.6.1 snapshot, 69,239 materials), JARVIS-DFT (2021.8.18 snapshot, 55,722 materials), and QM9 (130,829 molecules) datasets with their respective train/validation/test splits.

The plan includes the training procedure: MSE loss for regression and negative log-likelihood loss for classification, AdamW optimizer with weight decay 1e-5, batch size 64, and a one-cycle learning-rate schedule with maximum LR 0.001 for 300 epochs.
The plan includes reproducing the CFID (LightGBM) and CGCNN baselines on the JARVIS-DFT dataset using identical data splits to ALIGNN for a fair comparison.
The plan includes the layer-count ablation that varies the number of ALIGNN layers (0-4) and GCN layers (0-4) on the JARVIS-DFT formation energy and bandgap targets.
The plan includes ablations over hidden feature width, edge input feature size, embedding feature size, and node feature attribute set (CFID/atomic-number-only/CGCNN-type).
The plan includes the cross-validation learning-curve study that subsamples N_train training examples without replacement plus 5000 fixed validation examples, repeated over 5 CV iterates.

The plan notes that the default hyperparameters in Table 1 were originally selected via manual experimentation and Ray Tune-based random search on the JARVIS-DFT band gap validation task, and that reproduction can directly reuse the Table 1 values rather than repeating the search.
The code constructs a periodic crystal graph using a 12-nearest-neighbor rule per atom, extended to include all atoms within the full shell of the 12th-nearest neighbor.

The code assigns each atom node a feature vector derived from elemental properties such as electronegativity, group number, covalent radius, valence electron count, ionization energy, electron affinity, block, and atomic volume, consistent with the CGCNN-inspired feature set.
The code computes initial bond (edge) features as an RBF expansion of interatomic distances with cutoff support of 0-8 Å for crystalline structures.
The code computes initial bond (edge) features as an RBF expansion of interatomic distances with cutoff support up to 5 Å for molecular structures.
The code constructs the atomistic line graph L(g) in which each node corresponds one-to-one to a bond/edge of the atomistic graph g, and edges connect bond pairs sharing a common atom.
The code computes the bond-angle cosine theta = arccos((r_ij . r_jk)/(|r_ij||r_jk|)) for each atom triplet and expands it via RBF to obtain the initial line-graph edge features.
The code implements the edge-gated graph convolution node update h_i^(l+1) = h_i^l + SiLU(Norm(W_src^l h_i^l + sum_j e_ij^l W_dst^l h_j^l)) exactly as in Equation 2.
The code implements the normalized edge gate e_ij^l = sigma(e_ij^l) / (sum_k sigma(e_ik^l) + epsilon) exactly as in Equation 3.
The code implements the edge feature update e_ij^l = e_ij^(l-1) + SiLU(Norm(A^l h_i^(l-1) + B^l h_j^(l-1) + C^l e_ij^(l-1))) exactly as in Equation 4.
The code implements the equivalent coalesced-weight edge update using the augmented representation z_ij = h_i (+) h_j (+) e_ij and a single gating weight matrix W_gate, as in Equations 5-6.
The code implements the line-graph edge-gated convolution that produces bond messages m^l and updated triplet features t^l from the previous edge and triplet features, exactly as in Equation 7.
The code implements the bond-graph edge-gated convolution that consumes the bond messages m^l as edge input to update atom features h^l and bond features e^l, exactly as in Equation 8.
The code composes Equations 7 and 8 sequentially to form a single ALIGNN layer, sharing latent bond representations between the line-graph nodes and the bond-graph edges.
The code uses SiLU activations, rather than ReLU or Softplus, within every graph-convolution update.
The code stacks exactly 4 ALIGNN layers followed by 4 plain edge-gated GCN layers on the bond graph, matching the default configuration in Table 1.
The code applies global average pooling over the final atom/node representations before the output layer.
The code maps pooled representations to scalar outputs via a single fully connected layer, with separate output handling for regression and classification tasks.
The code sets edge input features=80, triplet input features=40, embedding features=64, and hidden features=256 for the default model configuration, matching Table 1.
The code applies batch normalization within the graph convolution updates as the default normalization scheme.
The code implements mean squared error (MSE) loss for regression targets and negative log-likelihood loss for classification targets.
The code implements training with the AdamW optimizer, weight decay of 1e-5, and a batch size of 64 for 300 epochs.
The code implements a one-cycle learning-rate schedule with a maximum learning rate of 0.001.
The code implements data loading and preprocessing for the Materials Project dataset (2018.6.1 snapshot, 69,239 materials) with a 60,000-5,000-4,239 train-validation-test split.
The code implements data loading and preprocessing for the JARVIS-DFT dataset (2021.8.18 snapshot, 55,722 materials) with an 80%:10%:10% train-validation-test split.
The code implements data loading and preprocessing for the QM9 dataset (130,829 molecules) with a 110,000-10,000-10,829 train-validation-test split.

The code implements threshold-based binarization of regression targets to produce the classification labels evaluated in Table 4 (e.g., metal/non-metal at 0.01 eV, magnetic/non-magnetic at 0.05 μB, ehull stability at 0.1 eV).
The code accesses the datasets via the JARVIS-Tools figshare module (or an equivalent reproducible mechanism) rather than recomputing the underlying DFT calculations.
The code ensures the held-out test split (10% for JARVIS-DFT, fixed test partitions for MP and QM9) is never used during training or during model/hyperparameter selection.
The code implements the CFID descriptor baseline trained with LightGBM for comparison on the JARVIS-DFT dataset.
The code implements/retrains the CGCNN baseline model on the JARVIS-DFT dataset using identical data splits to ALIGNN for fair comparison.
The code computes the mean absolute error (MAE) metric used to evaluate all regression targets.
The code computes the ROC AUC metric used to evaluate all classification targets in Table 4.
The code computes the mean absolute deviation (MAD) of each target and the MAD:MAE ratio reported alongside MAE in Tables 2 and 3.
The code supports configuring the number of ALIGNN layers and GCN layers independently in order to reproduce the layer-ablation grid in Table 6.
The code supports configuring hidden feature width, edge input feature size, and embedding feature size as independent hyperparameters for the model-analysis ablations.
The code supports swapping the node feature attribute set between CFID (438-dim), atomic-number-only (1-dim), and default CGCNN-type (92-dim) attributes.
The code supports swapping batch normalization for layer normalization as a drop-in replacement within the graph convolution layers.
The code implements per-epoch wall-clock timing instrumentation to compare computational cost across layer configurations and competing models.

The code implements the cross-validation learning-curve procedure: merging the standard JARVIS-DFT train/validation sets, then sampling N_train training examples without replacement plus 5000 fixed validation examples, repeated over 5 CV iterates.
The code reuses the same ALIGNN architecture and hyperparameters as the solid-state datasets for QM9, except trained for 1000 epochs instead of 300.
The ALIGNN training code has been executed successfully without errors on the Materials Project dataset for the formation energy (Ef) and band gap (Eg) targets.

The ALIGNN training code has been executed successfully without errors on the JARVIS-DFT dataset across its regression properties (or a representative core subset including formation energy, band gap, total energy, and ehull).
The ALIGNN classification training code has been executed successfully without errors on the JARVIS-DFT classification targets in Table 4.
The ALIGNN training code has been executed successfully without errors on the QM9 dataset for 1000 epochs.
The CFID and CGCNN baseline training code has been executed successfully without errors on the JARVIS-DFT dataset using identical splits.
The layer-count ablation runs (varying ALIGNN/GCN layers) have been executed successfully without errors to reproduce the Table 6 grid.
The cross-validation learning-curve experiment has been executed successfully without errors across multiple training set sizes and 5 CV iterates.
The hidden/edge/embedding feature-size and node-feature-type ablation runs have been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ALIGNN achieves the best formation energy MAE (~0.022 eV/atom) on the Materials Project dataset, outperforming CGCNN, MEGNet, and SchNet.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ALIGNN achieves the best band gap MAE (~0.218 eV) on the Materials Project dataset, outperforming CGCNN and MEGNet.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: ALIGNN achieves the lowest MAE among CFID, CGCNN, and ALIGNN across the majority of the 29 JARVIS-DFT properties, including formation energy (~0.033 eV/atom).
The execution outcomes match Table 3: ALIGNN outperforms CGCNN by more than 2x on total energy MAE.
The execution outcomes match Table 4: classification models achieve high ROC AUC scores, peaking at ~0.94 for the stable/unstable (ehull) classifier.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: ALIGNN outperforms SchNet, MEGNet, and DimeNet++ on the HOMO and dipole moment (mu) targets on QM9, while being comparable on other targets.
The execution outcomes match Table 6: removing all graph convolution layers (ALIGNN-0/GCN-0) increases formation energy MAE by roughly an order of magnitude relative to the default 4/4 configuration.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: the ALIGNN-2/GCN-2 configuration attains performance comparable to the default 4/4 configuration, while GCN-only configurations saturate at a clearly worse MAE (~0.044 eV/atom).

The execution outcomes match Fig. 3: configurations including at least two ALIGNN layers dominate the high-accuracy region of the accuracy-vs-compute pareto frontier relative to GCN-only configurations.

The execution outcomes match Fig. 4: validation MAE for the JARVIS-DFT formation energy task decreases monotonically with training set size, with no sign of saturation up to the full training set, reaching an average MAE of ~0.0316 eV/atom at the full size (44,577 examples).

The execution outcomes confirm that the default node-feature attribute set (CGCNN-type, 92 attributes) outperforms both CFID (438 attributes) and atomic-number-only (1 attribute) representations for the formation energy model.

The execution outcomes confirm that hidden feature width, edge input feature size, and embedding feature size each saturate in performance at or below the Table 1 default values (256, 80/40, 64/32 respectively).
The execution outcomes confirm that model performance converges by around 300 training epochs, with shorter training budgets giving modestly worse and more variable accuracy.
The execution outcomes confirm that switching from batch normalization to layer normalization yields a speedup with only a slight degradation in validation accuracy.

The execution outcomes confirm that ALIGNN requires fewer total training epochs (~300) than competing methods (DimeNet++, MEGNet, etc., which need ~1000+) to reach comparable accuracy, despite the higher per-epoch cost of ALIGNN.
The execution outcomes confirm that the CGCNN baseline retrained on JARVIS-DFT with identical splits is consistently outperformed by ALIGNN, validating the fairness of the comparison in Table 3.

The execution outcomes match the classification label thresholds in Table 4 (e.g., 0.01 eV for metal/non-metal, 0.1 eV for ehull stability), producing dataset label distributions consistent with the reported ROC AUCs.
